content:
The Oxford 2024 employment study tracked 800+ occupations across 47 countries. Here's what the data actually shows about AI and your job:

REPLACED (automation above 70%):
• Data entry clerks
• Basic bookkeepers
• Telemarketing
• Credit analysts doing standard assessments
• Tax prep for simple returns

These jobs follow clear rules. AI excels at rules.

AUGMENTED (automation 30-50%):
• Radiologists (AI spots anomalies, doctors diagnose)
• Paralegals (AI does doc review, lawyers strategize)
• Software developers (AI writes boilerplate, humans architect)
• Graphic designers (AI generates options, humans direct)

These jobs got faster, not extinct. The humans doing them shifted up the value chain.

THE 3 IRREPLACEABLE SKILL CATEGORIES:

1. Complex problem-solving in novel situations
Real example: A manufacturing engineer solving why a production line fails only on humid Tuesdays. No pattern exists for AI to learn from.

2. Genuine human connection
Hospice nurses, therapists, executive coaches. The study found 0% automation potential for roles where trust and emotional reading matter more than information transfer.

3. Creative strategy under uncertainty
CMOs deciding to kill a winning product, researchers asking questions nobody thought to ask. AI optimizes known games. Humans change the game.

YOUR MOVE:

The data shows job losses concentrated in the "follow the manual" layer. Growth happening in the "judgment call" layer.

Retraining isn't about learning to code. It's about moving from executioner to decision-maker in your field.

Panic scrolls LinkedIn. Strategy opens the spreadsheet of what skills your industry actually pays for in 2024 vs 2019.

Which category are you in? And more importantly, which are you moving toward?

Everyone's worried AI will take their job. But after studying this for months, here's what I found:

Three jobs AI actually can't crack yet—and the specific reasons why:

THERAPISTS
AI can pattern-match symptoms to treatments. It can't read the micro-expression when someone says "I'm fine" but their jaw tightens. It can't feel the shift in room energy when a client finally admits what they're ashamed of. Therapy works because another human witnessed your pain and stayed present. That's not a data problem—it's an embodied experience problem.

PLUMBERS
I watched my plumber last month diagnose a leak that wasn't where the water stain appeared. He felt temperature differences, listened to pipe sounds, noticed a floor slope that didn't match the house age. Every house is a unique mess of previous owners' choices, building code changes, and physics. AI needs predictable environments with clear rules. A 1947 bungalow with three amateur renovations is the opposite of that.

TRIAL LAWYERS
ChatGPT can draft a motion. It cannot read juror 7's face when the defendant testifies and realize she doesn't buy it. It can't decide in real-time to slow down and simplify because the jury looks confused. Persuasion isn't about perfect logic—it's about watching humans react and adjusting. You're performing for an audience that thinks in stories, not probabilities.

The pattern: AI struggles with physical unpredictability, real-time human reading, and embodied presence.

Which one do you think falls to AI first?

POLL:
- Therapists
- Plumbers  
- Trial lawyers
- None of them in the next 10 years

3 million jobs. Gone. Not "at risk" — already eliminated by AI automation.

Here's where they disappeared:

CUSTOMER SERVICE (1.2M jobs)
Klarna cut 700 customer service agents in 2024. Their AI chatbot now handles what those workers did. CEO said it out loud: equivalent work of the eliminated staff.
IBM stopped hiring for 7,800 back-office roles they say AI will do instead.
Duolingo laid off 10% of contractors doing customer support. Replaced with GPT-4.

DATA ENTRY (1.1M jobs)
This one's quiet because it happened at thousands of small companies nobody writes articles about. 
JP Morgan's COINnow reviews commercial loan agreements — work that consumed 360,000 hours of legal work annually.
Insurance processors at Fukoku Mutual Life in Japan? 34 employees replaced by IBM Watson in 2017.

CONTENT MODERATION (700K jobs)
Meta cut 21,000 jobs over 18 months. Not all moderation, but their AI now flags the majority of harmful content before human review.
TikTok's automated moderation jumped from 80% to 95%+ accuracy. Guess what happened to the review teams.

WHAT HAPPENED TO THOSE WORKERS?
This is the part nobody tracks systematically.
Some moved to "AI training" roles (usually lower pay, contract work).
Many aged 45+ left the workforce entirely.
The rest scattered into gig economy, retail, or are still looking.

The companies saved money. That part's documented in every earnings call.

The workers? We stopped counting them once they stopped being employees.

I'm not saying AI is bad. I'm saying we're pretending 3 million people is a rounding error when it's roughly the entire population of Chicago losing their jobs in under 5 years.

What's your company's plan for the people whose jobs get "optimized"?

I spent 3 hours reading the 274-page immigration bill. Here's what politicians said versus what's actually in it:

PROMISE 1: "This bill provides a clear pathway to citizenship for Dreamers"
REALITY: Page 89 excludes anyone with more than 2 misdemeanors, including traffic violations. Page 91 sets income requirements most Dreamers can't meet. The "pathway" has 47 separate eligibility requirements.

PROMISE 2: "We're ending family separation at the border"
REALITY: Page 156, Section 3402 expands mandatory detention categories to include parents of US citizen children. Page 158 removes judicial review for detention decisions under 100 days.

PROMISE 3: "Processing times will be cut in half"
REALITY: Page 203 adds 14 new documentary requirements. Page 217 mandates biometric screening that currently has 8-month backlogs. No new funding for processing staff (see page 245).

PROMISE 4: "This protects asylum seekers fleeing persecution"
REALITY: Page 134 creates a new "credible fear" standard requiring corroborating evidence within 48 hours of arrival. Page 138 bars asylum for anyone who transited through a third country, even if that country has no asylum system.

PROMISE 5: "Border security without a wall"
REALITY: Pages 67-72 authorize $4.7 billion for "physical barriers" and surveillance tech. Page 73 expands border enforcement zone from 25 to 100 miles inland, affecting 200 million Americans.

The gap between the speeches and the bill text isn't small. It's a canyon.

I'm not telling you what to think about immigration policy. But read the actual bill before you share the talking points. The people affected by this deserve that much.

PDF link in comments. Which promise-versus-reality gap surprised you most?
